questions.question: Explain the need for file handling in C programming. Why cannot we rely solely on variables for data storage?
keyPoints: Variables are stored in RAM which is volatile
Data in variables is lost when program terminates
File handling provides permanent storage on secondary storage
Files allow data persistence across multiple program executions

questions.question: What is a file pointer in C? Explain its role in file handling operations.
keyPoints: FILE pointer is a pointer to FILE structure
Contains information about file (name, buffer, position)
Returned by fopen() function
Used by all file I/O functions to identify the file

questions.question: Differentiate between text mode and binary mode of opening a file in C.
keyPoints: Text mode interprets special characters (like \n)
Binary mode stores data as raw bytes
Text mode may add/carriage return on some systems
Binary mode is portable and efficient for non-text data

questions.question: What is EOF? How is it used in file handling?
keyPoints: EOF stands for End of File
Defined as integer value -1
Returned by functions like fgetc() when file ends
Used in loops to read entire file content

questions.question: Describe the three standard file streams in C and their default connections.
keyPoints: stdin - standard input, connected to keyboard
stdout - standard output, connected to console/screen
stderr - standard error, connected to console/screen
These are automatically opened when program starts

questions.question: What is buffering in file I/O? Explain its advantages.
keyPoints: Buffering stores data temporarily in memory before I/O
Reduces number of system calls for read/write operations
Improves I/O efficiency and performance
Can be flushed explicitly using fflush()

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'r+', 'w+', and 'a+' file modes.
keyPoints: r+ - opens existing file for read+write, no truncation
w+ - creates new file or truncates existing for read+write
a+ - opens for read+append at end of file
All three allow both reading and writing

questions.question: Write a C program to open a file named "data.txt" in write mode, write the string "Hello World" to it, and then close the file. Include proper error handling.
keyPoints: Use fopen() with "w" mode
Check if fopen() returns NULL
Use fprintf() or fputs() to write data
Always use fclose() to close file
Include error messages for failed operations

questions.question: Explain the various file opening modes in C with examples of when each should be used. Include the behavior when the file exists and does not exist for each mode.
keyPoints: r - read only, file must exist
w - write, creates new or truncates existing
a - append, creates new or adds to end
r+ - read+write, file must exist
w+ - read+write, creates new or truncates
a+ - read+append, creates new or adds to end
Binary variants (rb, wb, ab, etc.)

questions.question: What is the importance of checking the return value of fopen()? Write a code snippet demonstrating proper error handling when opening a file fails.
keyPoints: fopen() returns NULL on failure
Always check for NULL before using file pointer
Failure reasons: file not found (r mode), permission denied, disk full
Proper error handling includes displaying message and exiting

questions.question: Explain the concept of file streams in C. How do they differ from file descriptors? Discuss the standard file streams and their typical usage.
keyPoints: File stream is a high-level abstraction using FILE structure
Provides buffered I/O operations
stdin, stdout, stderr are pre-opened standard streams
File descriptor is low-level integer handle
Streams offer more convenient functions than system calls
